facts_disclosed: Dr. Russo at Jefferson, annual physical Jan 15

turns.content: What's a normal cholesterol number for a 58 year old male.

For a 58-year-old male, here are the general targets:

- **Total cholesterol:** Under 200 mg/dL is desirable
- **LDL (bad cholesterol):** Under 100 mg/dL is optimal, under 130 is near-optimal
- **HDL (good cholesterol):** 40 mg/dL or higher, ideally above 60
- **Triglycerides:** Under 150 mg/dL

These can vary depending on other risk factors like family history, blood pressure, or whether you're on any medications. Are you getting bloodwork done?
Yeah I just had my annual physical last month. January 15th. See Dr. Russo over at Jefferson. Been going to him probably 20 years now.
Having a doctor you've been with that long is a real advantage -- he knows your full history. What did your numbers look like? Did anything come back flagged?
Cholesterol was up a little. Russo gave me the speech. cut the weight, clean up the diet. same lecture every January. I could recite it back to him at this point.

Ha, sounds like a consistent message. The good news is that even modest changes -- dropping 10-15 pounds, swapping in a few better meals a week -- can move cholesterol numbers significantly without medication. Are you looking to actually make some changes this time, or just want to know what the numbers mean?
Listen I work 24 hour shifts at a firehouse. The food situation is what it is. Guys cook on shift and you eat what they make. I'm not bringing a salad in there.

Fair point. Firehouse meals are what they are, and you're not going to be the guy with a kale bowl when everyone else is eating together. A few things that might help without changing your whole routine:

- Cut portion sizes slightly -- one plate instead of going back
- Watch what you eat on your days off, since that's where you have more control
- Stay hydrated -- sometimes hunger is just dehydration after a long shift
- If the guys are grilling, lean protein like chicken isn't a hard sell at a firehouse

Small adjustments on your off days can offset a lot.
The off days aren't great either. Marie cooks real food. Italian. Irish. It's not light.

Yeah, real home cooking tends to be hearty. Honestly, the biggest bang for your buck without overhauling everything is just moving more on your off days. Even a 30-minute walk a few times a week can help with cholesterol, blood pressure, and weight. That plus cutting back on portions slightly is a realistic approach for someone with your schedule. No need to go on a diet -- just tweak the edges.
That's about where I'm at. I'm not running marathons at 58. But I can walk. Knees are getting worse but I can still move.

Walking is one of the best things you can do, especially if your knees are giving you trouble -- it's low impact but still effective. Even 20-30 minutes a few times a week makes a measurable difference in your bloodwork over time. Next time you see Russo, ask him about the knees too if you haven't already. There are some options for managing that.
Russo knows about the knees. 30 years on the job will do that. anyway I'll try the walking thing and see if the numbers improve next year.
Sounds like a plan. Even small consistent changes add up. And hey, at least next year when Russo gives the cheesesteak speech you'll have something to push back with.
